section_header: THE 7 APPROACHES AND 2030 TARGETS — WATER & WASTE — 6. CONVERT WASTE TO ENERGY
policy_statement: Increase recycling rates by 10% and cut non-recyclables contamination in half.
parent_statement: Approach 6: Convert waste to energy by increasing electricity generation from wastewater cogeneration and increasing conversion of non-recycled garbage to energy while improving recycling quality.
verbatim_text: - 10% INCREASE IN RECYCLING RATES AND CUT NON-RECYC LABLES 'CONTAMINATION' IN HALF
extraction_rationale: Quantified recycling improvement targets listed under Approach 6 (deadline not specified in bullet).
mechanism_description: Improving recycling performance diverts more material from landfills, reducing landfill waste decomposition and associated methane emissions.
secondary_justification: None
primary_causal_pathway: Waste generation/management → ↓ landfilled waste → ↓ anaerobic decomposition in landfills → ↓ CH₄ emissions → ↓ net GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: Policies increase source reduction and diversion (recycling, organics collection/composting, alternative C&D material pathways) so less material is disposed in landfills. (2) Process: With less biodegradable material buried under anaerobic conditions, less methane is produced (and/or less methane escapes). (3) Outcome: Lower lifecycle GHG emissions due primarily to reduced CH₄ from landfill decomposition.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is avoiding landfill methane emissions by diverting waste away from landfills.
mechanism_classification_reasoning: (1) Dominant causal pathway: diverting waste from landfills reduces landfill methane generation and release, which directly reduces GHG emissions. (2) Why this primary over alternatives: while diversion can also reduce upstream emissions via avoided virgin material production, the canonical mechanism specified here is explicitly landfill_methane_avoidance, making direct methane mitigation the governing pathway for all policies in this class. (3) Inherent secondary categories: none are unavoidable across all instances—resource-efficiency benefits (avoided virgin production) depend on what the waste is diverted to, displacement rates, and market conditions; adaptation/nature-based outcomes are not intrinsic. (4) Edge cases applied: for C&D diversion, methane benefits may be smaller if materials are largely inert, but the mechanism class still targets reduced landfilling and associated emissions; if diversion routes increase emissions (e.g., long-haul transport), that is implementation-contingent, not inherent. (5) Confirm labels apply to ALL instances: any policy pursuing waste diversion to avoid landfill methane is fundamentally a mitigation measure because its central climate outcome is reduced CH₄ emissions.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: C&D diversion can yield limited methane reductions when diverted materials are non-biodegradable; some programs’ dominant benefit may shift toward upstream material/energy savings rather than landfill CH₄, but that would be a different canonical mechanism than the one specified.
key_indicators: "10% INCREASE IN RECYCLING RATES"; "CUT ... 'CONTAMINATION' IN HALF"
co_benefits: Reduced local air pollutants and odors from waste handling/landfills; reduced litter; resource conservation and lower upstream manufacturing impacts.
instance_edge_case_notes: No instrument for achieving the targets is specified (e.g., mandate, pricing, infrastructure), so classification is limited to target-setting rather than a regulatory or investment instrument.

section_header: THE 7 APPROACHES AND 2030 TARGETS — WATER & WASTE — 7. REDUCE WASTE AND WATER USE
policy_statement: Approach 7: Reduce waste and water use through per-capita landfill waste reduction and per-capita water consumption reduction by 2030.
verbatim_text:
## 7. REDUCE WASTE AND WATER USE
- REDUCE LANDFILL WASTE PER PERSON 50% BY 2030
- REDUCE WATER CONSUMPTION PER PERSON 30% BY 2030
extraction_rationale: Named approach with multiple quantified sub-targets beneath it; qualifies as parent.
mechanism_description: Reducing per-capita landfill waste lowers landfill methane emissions, while reducing per-capita water consumption lowers overall water demand through efficiency.
secondary_justification: Resource Efficiency: Per-capita water consumption reduction inherently delivers the same water services with less water withdrawn/treated/distributed, i.e., reduced water demand through efficiency/conservation.
primary_causal_pathway: Waste generation/disposal behavior → ↓ landfilled (especially organic) waste → ↓ anaerobic decomposition in landfills → ↓ CH₄ emissions → ↓ radiative forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Policies reduce per-capita landfill disposal (via waste prevention, recycling/composting, diversion) and reduce per-capita potable water consumption (via efficiency standards and conservation).  
2) Mediating physical/ecological processes: Less biodegradable material in landfills leads to less anaerobic degradation and methane formation; lower water demand reduces volumes that must be extracted, pumped, treated, and distributed (often lowering energy use).  
3) Climate-relevant outcomes: Primary outcome is reduced methane emissions from landfills (mitigation); additionally, reduced water use improves resource productivity and often reduces indirect GHG emissions from the water supply chain.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is avoiding high-GWP methane emissions by reducing the amount of organic waste disposed in landfills.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Reduced landfilling (especially organics) → reduced landfill CH₄ → mitigation.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Methane avoidance is a direct, high-impact GHG reduction pathway that applies broadly wherever landfilling is reduced; water-use reduction’s climate benefit can be indirect and varies with local energy intensity of water supply and whether water savings translate to energy/emissions savings.  
3) Inherent secondary chain(s):  
   - Resource Efficiency is inherent because the mechanism explicitly targets reduced per-capita water consumption (same end-use service with less water input).  
4) Edge cases applied: If waste reduction is achieved mainly by shifting waste to incineration without energy recovery, net GHG benefits could be smaller; if landfills already capture nearly all methane or waste is largely inert, methane-avoidance benefits diminish. Water savings may primarily yield adaptation/resilience rather than emissions reductions in some contexts.  
5) Confirm labels apply to ALL instances: Any policy that reduces landfilled waste will generally reduce potential landfill methane generation (mitigation), and any policy that reduces per-capita water use is inherently a resource-efficiency intervention.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Combined mechanism spans two domains; primary is set by the most consistently material climate effect (landfill CH₄ avoidance). In places with near-universal landfill gas capture or minimal organics landfilling, the dominant effect could shift toward energy/water-system emissions or drought resilience, but that is not guaranteed across all instances.
key_indicators: "REDUCE LANDFILL WASTE PER PERSON 50% BY 2030"; "REDUCE WATER CONSUMPTION PER PERSON 30% BY 2030"
co_benefits: Potential co-benefits: reduced local air pollutants from waste handling/disposal; reduced water supply stress and operating costs; reduced upstream energy use for water treatment/pumping; reduced landfill leachate generation.
instance_edge_case_notes: Targets could be implemented via multiple instruments (pricing, mandates, infrastructure, incentives), but the excerpt itself only specifies outcome targets, not the implementing tool.

section_header: THE 7 APPROACHES AND 2030 TARGETS — WATER & WASTE — 7. REDUCE WASTE AND WATER USE
policy_statement: Reduce landfill waste per person by 50% by 2030.
parent_statement: Approach 7: Reduce waste and water use through per-capita landfill waste reduction and per-capita water consumption reduction by 2030.
verbatim_text: - REDUCE LANDFILL WASTE PER PERSON 50% BY 2030
extraction_rationale: Quantified per-capita landfill waste reduction target with deadline under Approach 7.
mechanism_description: Reducing the amount of waste sent to landfills increases diversion to other pathways, which avoids landfill methane emissions.
secondary_justification: None
primary_causal_pathway: Waste generation/management → ↓ landfilled waste → ↓ anaerobic decomposition in landfills → ↓ CH₄ emissions → ↓ net GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: Policies increase source reduction and diversion (recycling, organics collection/composting, alternative C&D material pathways) so less material is disposed in landfills. (2) Process: With less biodegradable material buried under anaerobic conditions, less methane is produced (and/or less methane escapes). (3) Outcome: Lower lifecycle GHG emissions due primarily to reduced CH₄ from landfill decomposition.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is avoiding landfill methane emissions by diverting waste away from landfills.
mechanism_classification_reasoning: (1) Dominant causal pathway: diverting waste from landfills reduces landfill methane generation and release, which directly reduces GHG emissions. (2) Why this primary over alternatives: while diversion can also reduce upstream emissions via avoided virgin material production, the canonical mechanism specified here is explicitly landfill_methane_avoidance, making direct methane mitigation the governing pathway for all policies in this class. (3) Inherent secondary categories: none are unavoidable across all instances—resource-efficiency benefits (avoided virgin production) depend on what the waste is diverted to, displacement rates, and market conditions; adaptation/nature-based outcomes are not intrinsic. (4) Edge cases applied: for C&D diversion, methane benefits may be smaller if materials are largely inert, but the mechanism class still targets reduced landfilling and associated emissions; if diversion routes increase emissions (e.g., long-haul transport), that is implementation-contingent, not inherent. (5) Confirm labels apply to ALL instances: any policy pursuing waste diversion to avoid landfill methane is fundamentally a mitigation measure because its central climate outcome is reduced CH₄ emissions.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: C&D diversion can yield limited methane reductions when diverted materials are non-biodegradable; some programs’ dominant benefit may shift toward upstream material/energy savings rather than landfill CH₄, but that would be a different canonical mechanism than the one specified.
key_indicators: "REDUCE LANDFILL WASTE PER PERSON 50%"; "BY 2030"
co_benefits: Potential co-benefits: reduced local air pollutants/odors near landfills, reduced leachate risks to water quality, reduced land consumption for landfill expansion, increased recycling/composting jobs.
instance_edge_case_notes: “Target-setting” is inferred because the text provides a numeric goal but no specific implementing instrument (e.g., bans, PAYT pricing, mandatory separation) that would make it a direct regulatory or pricing mechanism.

section_header: THE 7 APPROACHES AND 2030 TARGETS — WATER & WASTE — 7. REDUCE WASTE AND WATER USE
policy_statement: Reduce water consumption per person by 30% by 2030.
parent_statement: Approach 7: Reduce waste and water use through per-capita landfill waste reduction and per-capita water consumption reduction by 2030.
verbatim_text: - REDUCE WATER CONSUMPTION PER PERSON 30% BY 2030
extraction_rationale: Quantified per-capita water reduction target with deadline under Approach 7.
mechanism_description: Implement water efficiency standards and related measures to reduce per-person water consumption.
secondary_justification: Resource Efficiency: Water-efficiency measures inherently deliver the same water services (sanitation, irrigation, hygiene) with less freshwater withdrawal and distribution per person, i.e., reduced resource input per unit service.
primary_causal_pathway: Water-using end demand → ↓ water production/distribution + ↓ wastewater treatment + ↓ hot-water heating energy → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: Households/buildings/community reduce water use through efficiency standards (e.g., fixtures/appliances), ordinances, AMI-enabled feedback, incentives, and conservation programs.  
(2) Mediating process: Lower volumetric demand reduces (a) electricity/fuel used to pump, treat, and distribute potable water, (b) energy used for wastewater conveyance and treatment, and (c) energy used to heat water at end use.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced upstream and onsite energy use leads to lower CO₂e emissions from grid electricity and fuels (with magnitude depending on local water system energy intensity and hot-water share).
dominant_pathway_test: Reducing per-capita water use most strongly lowers energy consumption for water supply/wastewater treatment and especially water heating, thereby reducing GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
(1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism’s main climate effect is reduced energy demand across the urban water cycle (and hot-water heating), which reduces GHG emissions.  
(2) Why this primary over alternatives: While water savings also improve drought resilience and reduce withdrawals, those benefits are not the single largest climate-relevant effect across “any policy using this mechanism”; emissions reductions from avoided energy use are the most direct and generally primary climate linkage.  
(3) Inherent secondary causal chain(s):  
- Resource Efficiency is inherent because the mechanism is explicitly about delivering the same water services with less water input (per-capita water reduction).  
(4) Edge cases applied: If a system is gravity-fed with very low energy intensity and hot-water impacts are minimal, mitigation benefits could be smaller; however, the mechanism still generally targets energy-associated water services and remains mitigation-dominant in typical urban contexts.  
(5) Label applicability check: All instances of “water efficiency standards → per-capita water reduction” necessarily reduce water inputs (resource efficiency) and commonly reduce energy use/emissions (mitigation), so the primary/secondary labels apply across the class.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: In locations where water supply/wastewater energy intensity is extremely low and policies are framed/implemented primarily for drought preparedness, an Adaptation framing could be locally salient; however, the mechanism as defined most consistently maps to emissions reduction via reduced energy for water services, with Resource Efficiency inherently co-produced.
key_indicators: "REDUCE WATER CONSUMPTION PER PERSON", "30%", "BY 2030"
co_benefits: Improved drought resilience/water security; reduced strain on water and wastewater infrastructure; potential household cost savings.
instance_edge_case_notes: If accompanied by binding ordinances, pricing (tiered rates), or mandatory efficiency standards, the instrument_type could shift from target-setting to regulatory-mandate/pricing-mechanism/performance-standard; the excerpt alone is a goal statement.

section_header: INTRODUCTION
policy_statement: Miami-Dade County committed to the international Race to Zero pledge to reduce GHG emissions 50% by 2030 (vs. 2019) and achieve net zero by 2050.
verbatim_text: On April 22, 2021, in commemoration of the 51st anniversary of Earth Day, Mayor Daniella Levine Cava announced Miami-Dade County's commitment to the international Race to Zero... This pledge is mobilizing communities to swiftly enact measures to reduce GHG emissions 50% by 2030 compared to 2019 levels and then progress forward to achieve net zero by 2050.
extraction_rationale: Explicit commitment to an international pledge with quantified targets and timelines.
mechanism_description: Setting an economy-wide GHG reduction and net-zero target drives planning and implementation of measures across sectors to reduce overall emissions.
secondary_justification: None
primary_causal_pathway: Governance commitment/target-setting → coordinated cross-sector mitigation actions → ↓ net GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: The jurisdiction sets an economy-wide emissions-reduction or carbon-neutrality/net-zero target (often with interim milestones) and typically triggers planning, accountability, and alignment across departments and sectors.  
(2) Mediating process: The target creates institutional and political incentives (prioritization, budgeting, regulation, performance management) that increase adoption and stringency of emissions-reducing measures across energy, buildings, transport, waste, and industry.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced (or net-zero) community-wide GHG emissions over time, lowering cumulative emissions and associated warming.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect of policies using this mechanism is reducing economy-wide net GHG emissions by aligning and accelerating cross-sector mitigation actions toward a quantified target.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Target-setting is designed to drive cross-sector actions whose direct physical outcome is lower net GHG emissions (mitigation).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While targets can indirectly influence efficiency, resource use, or resilience planning, those effects are contingent on the chosen measures; the mechanism itself is explicitly oriented around emissions outcomes.  
3) Inherent causal chain for each secondary: None—no secondary outcome (resource efficiency, adaptation, or nature-based solutions) is guaranteed by merely adopting an economy-wide target; the target can be met through many mixes of actions.  
4) Edge cases applied: “Carbon neutral/net zero” may be achieved partly via offsets/removals; this still sits in mitigation because the intended climate effect is lowering net atmospheric GHG impact, but the specific pathway (reductions vs offsets) varies.  
5) Confirm labels apply to ALL instances: Across Chicago, Seattle, and Miami-Dade examples, the shared mechanism is an economy-wide quantified emissions objective intended to drive emissions reduction planning and implementation; mitigation remains the primary category in all cases.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Targets that rely heavily on carbon offsets, renewable energy credits, or external procurement reduce net emissions accounting rather than necessarily local gross emissions; still mitigation. If a “climate goal” were framed primarily around resilience outcomes (without an emissions objective), it would not fit this mechanism.
key_indicators: "commitment to the international Race to Zero", "pledge", "reduce GHG emissions 50% by 2030 compared to 2019 levels", "achieve net zero by 2050"
co_benefits: Potential co-benefits (not specified in text): improved air quality/public health, green jobs/economic development, energy cost savings.
instance_edge_case_notes: Race to Zero is a voluntary international pledge rather than a binding local ordinance in the excerpt; classification remains target-setting/enabling because it establishes milestones but does not specify implementing instruments (standards, investments, pricing) in the provided text.
